Thanks a lot for this PR!!! I see a great potential for this idea, but I do have some thoughts and ideas.
In its current for, I do not per se see a lot benefits over your other PR (the docked plots #722), since the graphs are still practically three separated graphs but now with just a singular drop-down box for the x-axis. Feel free though to convince me otherwise (and I of course also don't speak for the other maintainers).

If, however, it would be possible to really merge the graphs (e.g. reduce/remove the space between the graph, only ticks and labels on the lower x-axis to save space, synchronised x-axes such that zooming/shifting in one axis works for all axes) I would see a tremendous advantage! (The holy grail in this sense I think would be to have the option to add or remove graphs on the go, but this might be (I don't know) extremely difficult).
This might be achievable using the  pyqtgraph.GraphicsLayoutWidget or the pyqtgraph.MultiPlotItem.

I do realize that this might be a bit (maybe a lot) more difficult than the current implementation, so I'm not sure if this is the right way to go, but just wanted to write down my thoughts on this.

Regardless of the outcome of the above suggestion, I added some first remarks (some of which might not be relevant if the above suggestion is followed)

Hey @ABD-01 and thanks for the continuing effort. I have some minor comments inline. Apart from them, two more things:

1. Please also add the new datasets to our documentation as shortly outlined in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/4255#pullrequestreview-732488800
2. I have not yet reviewed the `_get_people` and `_get_pairs` methods, as they really depend on the data format, which I don't know yet. However, this will be solved by the next point:

> The tests are remaining so I'll add them, could you please guide me on how to proceed with that?

Sure! You need to create two test cases, `LFWPeopleTestCase` and `LFWPairsTestCase`, in  [`tess_datasets.py`](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/test/test_datasets.py) that inherit from 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7947fc8fb38b1d3a2aca03f22a2e6a3caa63f2a0/test/datasets_utils.py#L180

Please read the docstring to get a better understanding what needs to be done. 

The absolute minimum that you need to provide, is an implementation of the `inject_fake_data` method. It gets passed a `tmp_dir` that needs to contain all files in the correct structure that would be present in `root` if I would instantiate the dataset with `LFWPeople(root, download=True)`. If the content changes depending on other keyword arguments, you can query them from the passed `config` dict, i.e. `config["train"]`. If you overwrite the class attribute `ADDITIONAL_CONFIGS` for example with 

```python
ADDITIONAL_CONFIGS = datasets_utils.combinations_grid(
    train=(True, False), image_set=("original", "funneled", "deepfunneled"),
)
```

you will get automate testing for all combinations. 

Of course the files provided by `inject_fake_data` musn't be the original files, but rather mock ups of them, i.e. "fake data". For example, you will want to create a `peopleDevTrain.txt` file, that only contains a few lines. For the accompanying images, you can use convenience utilities such as 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7947fc8fb38b1d3a2aca03f22a2e6a3caa63f2a0/test/datasets_utils.py#L666

or 

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/7947fc8fb38b1d3a2aca03f22a2e6a3caa63f2a0/test/datasets_utils.py#L702

For the LFW dataset, I propose you add a `_LFWTestCase` that handles the injection of fake data so that the actual test cases only need to handle their special stuff.

---

If all of the above is "too much", we can also do it step by step. Implement something and if you hit a roadblock, push the commit and ping me so I can take a look at it and guide you more precisely.

This doesn't include changes to schema generation, mentioned in #4360. 
https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/11d8589423ac82f64eb4007bdad824f4a6217434/pydantic/schema.py#L805


 So generating schema with re.Pattern most likely gonna blow up.

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that c.session.Accept() blocks until c.session.Close() is closed, which happens during c.Close(), but c.Close() also locks c.m, so we have to release it while c.session.Accept() runs, in order to avoid the deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered in go1.19 when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, the deadlocks would still have been happening, leaking deadlocked go routines, but functionality would not have been impacted, only performance.

A few comments as I have been using something like this for a couple of years.

> I don't know if this is supposed to be acceptable, but I thought it may at least be worth mentioning.

Maybe someday we could "translate" that constraint to "not null" in `tables.sql`, but for the moment I think it's fine.  I am surprised it's not showing up in `schema.md`, though.  #4063.

I agree that dropping the functions and using `if not exists` is awkward.  Failing to do that might leave "stale" migration functions around that would be run unexpectedly.  I suspect that the JS that's running these migrations should just remove the expected functions (if they exist) before calling the migrationScript and downgradeScript - then there's no need to worry about dropping them at all, nor of executing "stale" functions.  I'm happy to add that in a followup if you'd like - just file an issue.

This patch looks fantastic.  I agree that the tests are sufficiently thorough.  Maybe a good double-check would be to introduce bugs in the various redefined DB functions and check that the tests fail?

Good spot that as of the completion of version 0055's migrationScript, the table invariant is that provisioner_id/worker_type OR task_queue_id are set, or maybe both.  That means the redefined functions in 0055 must account for either of those possibilities -- as you've done.  That's definitely a subtle point, but I'm not sure how to make it less subtle..

One thing that's missing is to use the `state` to start the migration batch at the next taskId that needs attention.  As written, toward the end of the process the batch will spend most of its time scanning rows where the columns are already not null.

The other thing to change is to define the online downgrade functions in the `downgradeScript` parameter in version 0056, rather than `migrationScript`.  With that change, I think it makes a little more sense to have 0056's online downgrade be the "mirror image" of 0055's online migration, as it's more of a "followup to 0056's downgradeScript" mirroring the "followup to 0055's migrationScript".

Leaving the function changes to a second (third!) PR sounds fine.

Other than the inline comments, some other items could/should be addressed before finalizing this patch. Please let me know about your suggestions and if you can spot other shortcomings.

1. Special attention should be given to the new SQL Compilation Caching feature [1,2]. The `CrateDialect` should `supports_statement_cache = True`, see [3]. Also, there is a new `cache_ok` attribute on custom types [4], which should probably be applied to all CrateDB SQLAlchemy types listed in `crate.client.sqlalchemy.types`. [5] might also be related here.

   Currently, we observe all those warnings referenced at SA's [identify what constructs are blocking caching from being enabled](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/faq/performance.html#step-two-identify-what-constructs-are-blocking-caching-from-being-enabled) documentation:
   > SAWarning: Dialect crate:crate-python will not make use of SQL compilation caching as it does not set the `supports_statement_cache` attribute to `True`.  This can have significant performance implications including some performance degradations in comparison to prior SQLAlchemy versions.  Dialect maintainers should seek to set this attribute to True after appropriate development and testing for SQLAlchemy 1.4 caching support.   Alternatively, this attribute may be set to `False` which will disable this warning. (Background on this error at: https://sqlalche.me/e/14/cprf)

   > SAWarning: UserDefinedType `{_Craty(),_ObjectArray(),Geopoint(),Geoshape()}` will not produce a cache key because the ``cache_ok`` attribute is not set to `True`.  This can have significant performance implications including some performance degradations in comparison to prior SQLAlchemy versions.  Set this attribute to `True` if this type object's state is safe to use in a cache key, or `False` to disable this warning. (Background on this error at: https://sqlalche.me/e/14/cprf)

   > SAWarning: `{Class Any,Class Match}` will not make use of SQL compilation caching as it does not set the `inherit_cache` attribute to `True`.  This can have significant performance implications including some performance degradations in comparison to prior SQLAlchemy versions.  Set this attribute to `True` if this object can make use of the cache key generated by the superclass.  Alternatively, this attribute may be set to `False` which will disable this warning. (Background on this error at: https://sqlalche.me/e/14/cprf)

I would argue that this is a sensible work-around for the situation that django-auditlog finds itself in now, an era where there is an actual JSONField in Django instead of the pseudo JSONField from django-jsonfield.

`additional_data` hasn't really been used much in the app I think, so I don't think we should over-optimize by switching to Django's JSONField but treat it as a dumb field to store data in.

Of course alternatively we could switch to [django-jsonfield-backport](https://pypi.org/project/django-jsonfield-backport/) and actually convert all the data from the TextField to an honest JSON field but that would be a pretty big data migration for most installations given the purpose of the app.

Thanks Shreyas for raising this PR and adding a test! But I think this is intentional since the last refactor. The intended behavior is that once an HTTP proxy is in unhealthy state, it will be killed and restart a new one. When this happens, the new proxy should have zero failure count again. Thanks to @jjyao's [note](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/serve/_private/http_state.py#L186), it clearly states the final state for proxies are `UNHEALTHY` or `DRAINED`. 


Although this change won't really do anything, I don't think it fixes the issue you are facing. Just to help understand this flow a bit better and find the root cause. Maybe review some of these code?
1. [HTTPProxyStateManager#update](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/serve/_private/http_state.py#L365) This was called from controller's event loop. It goes through each proxy state to update. Then in the end it kills the unwanted ones. Then it starts new ones on the nodes that needs it.
2. [HTTPProxyState#update](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/serve/_private/http_state.py#L183) Everything happening can be summarized in the state machine comment.
3. [HTTPProxyStateManager#_stop_proxies_if_needed](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/serve/_private/http_state.py#L503) It goes through all the proxy states and shutdown the ones that's on inactive nodes, unhealthy, or drained. 
4. [HTTPProxyStateManager#_start_proxies_if_needed](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/serve/_private/http_state.py#L477) It goes through all the active nodes and start new instance of proxy/ proxy actor and wrap it in a new instance of `HTTPProxyState`. Since this is a new instance, it should start with `STARTING` state and have zero failure count again.


Hope this helps a bit. Let me know if you want to pair some time on Monday to dig deeper into any of this.

I think this is a great opportunity for introing asset factories as a whole, with a specific YAML starting point. A nice formalization of https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/discussions/11045.

(In fact, I'd go a far as to say approaching this as an asset factory example that happens to use YAML would be more valuable than a bespoke YAML DSL example).

A few thoughts:

- It would be great to flush out the README or create an "Assets Factory" guide. I think our examples often lack the prose to be approachable to newer users. But maybe thats a follow on to the RFC

- In terms of requirements, I think three things come to mind that aren't immediately obvious from this example:

(1) How would a non-technical user define these yaml specs and have dagster pick them up? This challenge is probably more bespoke to each org, but I think users often assume, want, or get confused about whether this YAML-loading can be done periodically, almost as a runtime thing, instead of as a build time step. They are used to a tool like Airflow "picking up" whatever files land in a dag directory in blob storage. In other words, if the person writing the YAML is totally separate from the Dagster team, what does the handoff look like? That'd be worth flushing out in a guide.

(2) Often the YAML-writer is interested in parameterizing a templated pipeline - or another way to think about it, often the YAML is at higher level than the asset graph, so one YAML chunk might represent a series of assets, not the 1:1 relationship shown here. How should users think about best representing these scenarios? Should the YAML translate to job-configuration for a pre-defined asset job? Or should the YAML "parameters" lead to the creation of a "copy" or "instantiation" of the templated assets? eg

(a) person A provides in YAML a list of stock tickers
(b) Each stock ticker should become an asset
(c) A final downstream asset should depend on all of (b)
(d) person B provides a different YAML list of stock tickers

(3) A follow up to 2, but perhaps the example could include a multi-asset as well? A common use case for asset factories is when some some job or sql snippet is run that results in an unknown # of assets or source assets being created.  It might be useful for this example to show how a multi-asset could be created for that case, and then how a downstream asset could depend on the dynamic upstreams



In short, the TL;DR is that this looks great, but might be worth considering:

What if the YAML users aren't writing YAML in the dagster repo? and What if their YAML is not a direct 1:1 with dagster assets?

I'd like @tacastillo 's perspective too, since he has long been an asset factory advocate.

Got a general suggestion on how you could tweak this functionality slightly, so I'll save any small review points for now...

As I understand it this is piggybacking on the existing language version functionality, so using `owner` and differentiating the versions by number and then getting the latest version for a language (if enabled) in the middleware.

However, I think there's an important distinction between the language versions and "content" versions of pages. We only know what language a user wants at the point a request is made, so we have to look for the correct version in the middleware. With content versions we already know which version we should show to the user before the request is made because we don't care about old versions (except for staff members).

There might be another way of handling it but here is my suggestion:
Add another foreign key relation to `page` on the `Page` model and call it something like `version_for`, keep `version` for ordering purposes and `verbose_name` `owner` to something more language specific. Then in the `PageManager` by default we can filter out any pages that have `version_for` set and we can either add another middleware class `VersioningMiddleware` or extend `PublicationMiddleware` to serve up different versions on the frontend for admins.

Caveats to the above:
- It should only be possible to make versions for pages that don't have `version_for` and `owner` set, essentially just the root page.
- Content will need to be copied over when making a page the 'live' version (or you could shuffle the foreign keys to make it the root object but that seems more sketchy)
- When updating a language version it will have to look at its owner's `version_set`

I think I see what's going on here. There's a race condition between `await semaphore.acquire()` in the `while True` loop and the actual scheduling of the task and its associated callback to `release` the semaphore. When the consumer is cancelled it may still be holding the lock but tries to wait on itself, thus the deadlock.

Something like this should work in theory, using `asyncio.shield` to make the `acquire` and `add_done_callback` block essentially guaranteed to run uninterrupted by cancellation:

```python
async def consumer(...):
    try:
        semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_tasks)

        async def create_task() -> None:
            await semaphore.acquire()
            # the rest of the code
            task.add_done_callback(lambda _f: semaphore.release())
            message_queue.task_done()

        while True:
            await asyncio.shield(create_task())
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        # exception handling code as you wrote it previously
```

This is certainly a good fix for the read + map_batches fusion case! However, I think doing something like the following, with an outer iteration over the input blocks, would be a cleaner implementation:
```python
        def transform(
            blocks: Iterable[Block],
            batch_fn: BatchUDF,
            *fn_args,
            **fn_kwargs,
        ) -> Iterable[Block]:
            DatasetContext._set_current(context)
            output_buffer = BlockOutputBuffer(None, context.target_max_block_size)
            # Ensure that zero-copy batch views are copied so mutating UDFs don't error.
            batcher = Batcher(batch_size, ensure_copy=batch_size is not None)

            def process_next_batch() -> Iterator[Block]:
                batch = batcher.next_batch()
                # Convert to batch format.
                batch = BlockAccessor.for_block(batch).to_batch_format(batch_format)
                # Apply UDF.
                batch = batch_fn(batch, *fn_args, **fn_kwargs)
                if not (
                    isinstance(batch, list)
                    or isinstance(batch, pa.Table)
                    or isinstance(batch, np.ndarray)
                    or (
                        isinstance(batch, dict)
                        and all(isinstance(col, np.ndarray) for col in batch.values())
                    )
                    or isinstance(batch, pd.core.frame.DataFrame)
                ):
                    raise ValueError(
                        "The map batches UDF returned the value "
                        f"{batch} of type {type(batch)}, "
                        "which is not allowed. "
                        f"The return type must be one of: {BatchType}"
                    )
                # Add output batch to output buffer.
                output_buffer.add_batch(batch)
                if output_buffer.has_next():
                    yield output_buffer.next()

            # Process batches for each block.
            for block in blocks:
                batcher.add(block)
                while batcher.has_batch():
                    yield from process_next_batch()

            # Process any partial/remainder batches.
            batcher.done_adding()
            if batcher.has_any():
                yield from process_next_batch()

            # Yield partial/remainder blocks from finalized output buffer.
            output_buffer.finalize()
            if output_buffer.has_next():
                yield output_buffer.next()
```

Hi @ponopono0322, thank you for opening a PR! This is really great, and seems to get us 90% of the way there 🙂

My tracking issue wasn't specific enough though, so there's a slight change we need to make. Mostly LGTM, the big thing that we need resolve IMO is the semi-lazy read behavior. Semi-lazy reading (eagerly reading the first block/file) has been confusing for users, results in redundant reading when there's stage fusion or conversion to a pipeline, and I think we should try to get rid of it when switching to lazy execution by default, if possible. I think that it would be much better if we had fully lazy mode and eager mode, where semi-lazy execution is an execution optimization that is enabled when we determine we only need to compute a subset of blocks; these semantics should translate pretty well to our new execution planner and streaming execution model (e.g. limit pushdown, metadata peeking, streaming consumption of narrow op chains).

Instead of always computing the first block, I think that we should make the read fully lazy, and if the user immediately calls `ds.schema()` right after reading, we only then trigger reading of the first block (and only if the schema isn't already available from e.g. file metadata).

I _think_ it should be pretty straightforward to move this `lazy_block_list.compute_first_block()`/progressive computation logic to the `ExecutionPlan`, since we can have `ExecutionPlan.schema()` (and other ops that only require the first block) trigger semi-lazy execution with e.g. a certain flag set, and if that flag is set AND the plan only consists of a read stage AND the input blocks are a lazy block list, we trigger minimal computation of that read stage.
